sentences.text: Yes.
And we left, actually-- we didn't take anything with us, because you couldn't.
You know, there was no way, because you have to rely just on, on your legs.
Just on-- And eventually, you could get maybe a little bit transportation, if, if, if you have a chance.
But most of the time, you didn't get that chance.
And I took some money.
And my brother took more because he was actually just by himself.
He was more, more organized.
And people used to-- in each town, the Jewish people would have special organizations.
They would feed those people and would, a little bit, finance, because they knew that we are without anything.
And finally, we got to Bialystok, which is actually a larger city, and farther from Warsaw, and more to the east.
And it was very tough, because you could see people killed.
Because all the time, you could-- those, those Nazi planes were flying very low.
Whatever they saw, a populated area, they would shoot down.
And you, all the time, were running away.
You have to fight for your life.
Finally, we came to, to Bialystock.
I remember my shoes, you know, I had one pair.
I went into-- to fix.
They fixed my shoes.
As soon as I came out from the, from the shoe company-- it was the Czechoslovakian company, Bata, B-A-T-A-- and all of a sudden, there's a, it was an alarm.
And people got to hide.
And-- and-- and they bombed.
I was very close where a bomb fell down.
I remember, my face was a little bit-- you know, it was, it was blood.
Some little pieces.

But nothing happened, nothing special, because I could see people without any legs, and terrible, terrible-- And-- and they had problems even to-- to take those people to the hospital, because it was so many, and they didn't have the transportation.
And then, what I found out, that that company, the shoe business, was bombed.
And the whole crew got killed.
If they had finished my shoes a little bit-- let's say a minute or two minutes later-- I wouldn't have a chance.
I would have-- because, because of that alarm, I would have to stay in that store, and I would get killed.
This was the first chance that I got alive.
I got some more occasions like this.
And again, Jewish organizations, you used to feed us and give us some money.
And we still start to go farther to the east.
And in the meantime, what happened that the Soviet government made an agreement with the-- with Hitler's government to divide Poland in two.
The first, the first side, they-- they made an agreement to occupy and the Soviet Union to occupy the east side.
Then, we were already-- and this happened on the 17th of September.
And we were, and then we were among-- you know, from one side, the Germans were coming from the west to east, and the Russian army used to come from east to the west.
And they established the border on the river Bug, B-U-G.
Yeah.
And then, then it was a little bit easier, because it was no bombardments, because we were on the Soviet side.
And I-- and of course, I used to meet people from my town.
I would ask about my brother.
And finally, I-- a son of my brother's boss met me.
And he said, you're here and your brother is looking for you.
He is in the Rowno.
R-O-W-N-O.
And I was rushing, of course.
It was no, no danger because no bombing already.
And I went to Rowno, and I, and I met my brother working for a, for a, for a watch maker.
Again?
I was without him for about, I would say, 10 days.
Then we joined together.
And of course, he, he could make money, because he was a watchmaker.
I am a book keeper.
Nobody-- he supported me.
Of course, at that time, you know, it was normal.
Anyway, he was a good man and a good brother.
But it bothers him that he was in, in Rowno and making money.
And his wife is on the German side.
And she, and she was in the late pregnancy.
You understand?
And then, after the establishment between the German government and the Soviet, people used to travel both sides, you know?
And my-- and we went back, back to Bialystock, because my brother want to, wanted to cross the border and go back to Warsaw and to see his wife, and eventually the baby.
And when we came to the border-- which means, it was not so simple, because you needed a guide and to pay him.
He kept us in the, in the, in the in the forest during the night.
It was cold.
But he used to bring tea, hot tea.
And finally, he said, listen, I will go to visit the parents and my wife.
And I will come back.
Then, it's no way-- for what-- for you have to  go.
You're single.
You stay, and I will come back.
And he went to Warsaw.
Yes.
And saw his wife, and the pregnant wife, and my parents.
And he came back with the middle brother.
Nissan, yes.
And we came back, and we stayed in Bialystock, which was also on the Soviet side.
And then, those the Russians, you know, they had a lot of money, but they didn't have anything.
You know, very poor clothing, you know?
And they wanted just watches, watches.
They used to call it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And you could give them anything because they didn't understand.
The didn't-- and they would pay any price.
And we used to make a little money on the side because my brother.
He used to stay in the store.
He would give me those watches with no big value, and I would be on that little market.
And they would come and buy from me, yeah, those German soldiers or the civilian, civilians, civilians who came together with the, with the army.
Yes.
Yes,
Well--
Oh, yes.
Just young people.
The elderly people wouldn't, wouldn't leave.
No.
She couldn't.
She couldn't They didn't ask for the women.
Just, they said, very-- they said very definitely, just men, young men.
That age up to 30.
No.
I used to go-- I knew that I'm going east.
But where I'm going-- But you see, when you were young, I don't think too much about it.
Now, I, I, I, I'm thinking back, and what I-- how did I go?
How could I go?
But when you're young, you don't, you know?
You'll do anything.
They told you to go, you go, you know?
And you're not afraid of anything.
When?
No, no.
Bialystock-- you see, and then what happened is another story.
[PHONE RINGING] Oh.
You see, what happened again is this.
[PHONE RINGING] The Soviet Union-- [PHONE RINGING] The Soviet, Soviet, Soviet government asked about registration.
They wanted to know about the people, whether they want to become Soviet citizens or to go back to the Hitler side.
Of course, we were very sensitive.
To us, our family, it means a lot.
And regardless what's going to happen, we registered to go back.
And, of course, we didn't know that Hitler was going to annihilate all the Jewish people.
We knew that it's not good.
It's bad.
That-- we knew that Hitler's pogram was very, very dangerous.
But still, you know?
And we decided, decided to go again because my brother was already in the war.
Then, we decided, all three of us, to go back to the parents.
But we decided, decided to make it in two tour, in two parts.
The middle brother, he wanted to be first.
And he went first.
And at that time, you had already borders with those-- You couldn't cross it.
How you call it, those wires?
You know?
And he got a good guy-- a man-- and he got into Warsaw.
By paying?
What?
Yes.
Sure.
Yeah.
I would say, like today, $20.
Or maybe less.
It's a long time back, you know?
And this was actually in 1940.
And then, and then, when I went with my brother, and we stayed a few nights in the forest because the guides would say, no, this night is not going to cross it.
You have to wait when it will be really the right time.
So the guards helped you?
Sure.
You couldn't do it without a guide who knew the territory around where the border was located.
And as soon we crossed the wires, all of a sudden, you know, Jude, Jude, Jude!
The German soldiers, Jude, which means Jew.
Jude!
Jude!
And right away, they took us, and they started to make fun of us.
You lay down, you get up, you lay down.
But we were lucky that those were not the real Gestapo.
They were just plain soldiers, elderly people.
And they kept us, over the-- during daytime, in a little place.
And--
And they didn't give us-- what?
It's like in the country, where they keep the cows or the horse, you know?
In a place like this.
During the daytime, they took out everything from us.
They didn't give us any food.
And at night, they took us again to the border and said to cross it to the, to the Soviet side.
And they warned us, if they will catch us again, they will kill us.
And now, again, we were on the Soviet side and back, back to Bialystock.
And my brother used to make a little money because he was the watch maker.
And it was a very terrible situation because we used to get news, what's going on on the Hitler side, the-- the-- the, you know, the killing.
You know, whatever is in the books and the papers.
And we were in Bialystock for a short time.
This was about June, 1940.
And then, you know, the Soviet soldiers used to look for those guys who registered that they want to go back, back to Warsaw-- to-- not to Warsaw, for the German side.
Yes.
And we were hiding, but they caught us, and put us, right away, through the police station.
In-- also in a, in a little place where you keep like, bicycles, you know?
And-- how you call this?
A storage room, yes.
We were hiding with a little bed, or we were sleeping on the, on the ground.
But they caught us, those, those Russians, and took us to the police stations.
As soon as they took us to the police, they separated us, immediately, me and my brother.
And, of course, in the police stations, we were in the hundreds of young men like we were.
And after-- I don't remember-- a day or two, they took myself, me.
I didn't know anything about my brother.
I lost already my brother.
And they took us to a church where they took out everything from the church, just left all the walls.
And they put in, over there, I believe at that time it was about 1,200 people.
And the place was maybe, the most, for for 400, for just to sit down.
Even at night, you couldn't find a room just for your legs and for your head.
So-- and also, they wouldn't let you get out.
They got those special boxes, like here for, for garbage.
You know, those-- and this was the-- forgive me-- the toilet.
But it was locked, you know?
And around there, it was terrible.
A lot of-- it was wet.
And they used to bring, twice a day, a little piece of bread and a little soap.
And they kept us in that, in that church, I believe it was at least two months, in the terrible conditions.
Once they asked us to go out-- and I will talk about [? myself-- ?] they take picture.
Picture and face, like this, and the full face.
The big-- we were those criminals, you know?
For--
[LAUGHS] And I remember, on the way back, I looked around.
He hit me with his, with his boot, the Russian soldier.
And you know, we were young.
I, you know-- I, you didn't-- what happened.

And then, after two months, they took us-- everything with guns because we were, for them, criminals, because we wanted to go back to the, to the Hitler side-- and they put us into cars, railroad cars like Hitler's.
And they locked.
And they put in also three times as many people as they could.
And made a little-- forgive me-- a hole in the cart, and this was the toilet.
And locked completely.
Bialystock.
Oh, the time was summer.
It was about August.
Oh, sure.
Terribly hot, terribly hot.
And they even arrested, you know, some families, father, and mother, and kids.
Yeah.
And we were in that cars for about two days or three days.
And then, they took us through-- and the traveling was about three, four months.
Until they took us very far north, not far from Finland.
It was about 400 miles from Finland, that was-- later I found out-- where the weather was very cold.
Nine months, it was sometimes 50 below zero.
Snow about three, four feet.
And, and, and many people died during that trip because they couldn't make it.
Sometimes they wouldn't even-- for a day, for a day, they wouldn't give us at all any food, any water.
And constantly, in the middle of the night, when the trains, they stopped, they came in and count whether they got the right number of people.
And again, they locked.
And also, they were walking on the roofs with a-- to look where there's no opening because somebody's trying to, to run away.
And this took about four months.
Because at that time, it was the war time.
It-- at that time, 19-- no, it was in 1940.
Yeah, in 19-- what exactly?
And it-- we didn't have any priority.
You know, the other cars, let's say, with some merchandise or passengers, they were first.
And they kept, kept us on the side.
And we arrived to the Komi, which is a-- Komi, K-O-M-I, a part of the Soviet Union.
But it's-- very few people lived there because it's impossible.
And they, they were keeping just those, those, those criminals, like criminals, like, say, an apostrophe, because we were not criminals.
And when they let us out, it was no place to live.
They said, OK, we have forest.
Cut the trees and build, build yourself where to live.
And we used to put those, you know, those trees like, this way, and some, some ground in between.
And the-- and that's the way we used to live inside.
But-- but it was not cold because we got plenty of, of wood.
And the-- the-- the fireplace was like, you keep those kerosene.
And this was the oven-- it was not cold.
But we used to go out to work in the-- around-- five-- they used to wake us up at around, let's say, 5:00 in the morning.
And we used to have a soldier with a rifle.
And he used to say, every, every morning, the same thing.
If you will move to the right or to the-- we used to go out in pairs.
And if you will move to the left or to the right, you will get killed.
You have to go straight.
And we used to cut those, those trees.
What?
Yeah.
Maybe I'm going too--
No, I didn't know where I am.
I knew that I'm in a very, place where it's very cold, a lot of snow, and the nights are like daytime because it's close, close to the North Pole.
Yes.
Oh, we got to build a place where to live.
We cut those trees, cut the branches.
Oh, they used to give us those-- of course, they gave us the saws.
So-- and I don't know how you call it.
What you cut.
Yeah.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
And this was-- and this was the first place where they used to-- we used to live there.
And then, again, they moved us to a real barracks, where it was a little bit better built, and built a little bit-- a place where to sleep.
A little bit-- let's say about two feet higher high.
But one row, which means each slept close to each other, about 100 people.
And covered with whatever you got, those [? rocks. ?] This was your cover in-- at night.
No.
Inside was not cold because we used to have that, that wood.
And somebody used to take care to keep the place warm inside.
Outside, it was very cold.
You spit, and you could see clear and the-- becoming a piece of, of ice.
A lot of snow.
And the temperature, as I told you, used to be up to 50, 55 below zero.
Very cold.
First of all, we were mixed with the Russian's criminals, real criminals.
Because over there, everybody tells the truth.
One would say, I killed, you know, wife.
I killed-- I was a-- it was a hold up.
And it was a lot of them were people with professions.
Because in 1937, Hitler, you know, was afraid of his power.
And he, he would, you know, he had at least the shade of some information that somebody told, told about somebody, that he's against the revolution.
And you could see a lot of lawyers and doctors in those camps.
And, of course, real criminal, criminals, would say exactly they are criminal.
Yeah.
They were criminals.
We were, you know, we were afraid of them even, you know?
Because they were dominating here.
We were people, you know family people.
And used to stay with the parents, and knew that we have to-- come Sabbath, you go to the synagogue.
And, and it was-- you know, it's so many years back that it's even tough to explain.
It was real, real, real bad.
Un-human conditions.
You know, you can not-- you know what happened also, that they-- we used to work seven days a week.
And they used-- we used to have horses to carry, you know, those three-- They gave the horses a day of rest.
The horses used to work six days a week, and we used to work seven days a week.
A typical day was that, let's say, you went to sleep, covered with those rocks, whatever.
I never saw, in my life, even being in Europe, being in Africa, covered with such rocks that I was.
Not just myself, all my, all our people.
And they used to wake up.
Of course, it was very tough to get up because we were working like, you know, like an animal.
Animals maybe doesn't work so hard.
And we used to get, in the morning, hot water.
Just hot water.
They call it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Just hot water.
Because the main dish was-- when you used to come back from the work, from the woods, they would give you 400 grams bread.
Which means 250 was maybe water and a little bit soup.
Because we couldn't make-- they, they would require from us to do more work, a lot of work.
But who could do it?
Then we used to get the minimum.
Let's say those Russian fellows, they, they could withstand those, the terrible situations.
They could do anything.
And they used to get more because they could do more work.
We were, you know, not used  to it.
And we used to get just 400 gram bread and a little soup.
You couldn't hold even a little piece for the next day.
You, you, you ate, you, you eat the whole thing at once, and the soup.
Which means, in the morning, you had just the, just the water to drink.
And at work, the hunger used to come to you-- the hunger used to come to you and back, come and back.
It was a terrible feeling.
You were hungry, and you have nothing to put in your mouth.
Sometimes we could find, under the snow-- because the summer is there three months.
We used to find something, let's say, some leaves or whatever.
And that's what we take, took into our mouth.
Yeah.
The leaves, yeah.
And then, we-- and it used to be a long way to go to the place where we used to cut-- from the barracks to the place where we used to cut those trees, it was a long way even to walk.
And some people, because no nutrition, at night, they used to get the-- they were blind.
And as soon as got dark, they were blind.
Maybe you've heard about?
They couldn't-- you know?
And we used to have to carry them because they were completely without any nutrition.
No sugar, no butter, no fat, no, no meat, no nothing.
Just a piece of bread and soup.
